preFix.title: Applying masonry waterproofing cream
preFix.subtitle: Stop rainwater penetration through exposed brickwork or render
preFix.excerpt: How to apply a silicone-based waterproofing cream to external brickwork or render to prevent driving rain from soaking through the wall, covering surface preparation, application method, and the invisible finish the cream leaves.
preFix.sourceNotes: BRE Good Repair Guide on rain penetration through external walls. BS 6477: water repellents for masonry surfaces. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

content.text: is one of the simpler external wall treatments. Applied to dry brickwork or render, it penetrates the masonry surface and makes it

but water-resistant. The cream soaks in and leaves no visible film; the brickwork looks the same colour as before once dry. It is not a cure for structural cracks, pointing failures, or rising damp: those need their own remedies first.

Silicone cream waterproofer (Stormdry Masonry Protection Cream, Ronseal Wet Rot Wood Hardener and Crack Filler, or any solvent-free cream waterproofer with a published WTA Merkblatt 4-02 or BS 6477 certification). One coat is the standard application; some products require two coats on very porous surfaces. For cracks wider than 1 mm, use exterior masonry filler before applying the cream. Masking tape to protect window frames and door surrounds.
Step 1, check and prepare the masonry surface

The wall must be dry before you apply the cream: wet masonry cannot absorb the silicone. Check the weather forecast and pick a dry spell of at least two days with no frost in the forecast. Brush off any algae, lichen, or loose render with the 50 mm brush. Fill cracks wider than 1 mm with an exterior flexible filler and allow to dry fully. Repoint any failed mortar joints before treating the wall. The cream cannot bridge open joints, and any untreated crack will remain a water entry point regardless of the surrounding treated area.

Mask window frames, door surrounds, and any painted render or cladding adjacent to the brickwork to be treated. The silicone cream stains painted surfaces and is difficult to remove once it soaks in. Cover window sills and any paths or planted beds below the work area with dust sheets: the cream runs when applied in volume.

Apply the cream generously with the 50 mm brush, working from the top of the wall downward. Work the cream into the mortar joints and onto the brick or block face with a scrubbing motion. The surface should look wet and saturated. Do not try to stretch the cream over a large area with light brushing: the surface must be fully loaded. Apply until the cream stops soaking in and begins to form a slight surface wet film, then stop. That is the saturation point.

Work in sections of about 2 m2 at a time. Join each section to the last with a wet edge to avoid lap marks. On very porous brick (old London stock, handmade brick, some lightweight block), a second coat after the first has dried (typically two to four hours) gives better protection. Allow the wall to dry for 24 hours before rain.

For large areas of plain rendered wall, a paint roller (9-inch medium pile) is faster than a brush. Roll in a W pattern and work the cream into the surface with the roller on a second pass. The roller does not reach into mortar joints as effectively as a brush; use the brush to work cream into the joints first, then roll the face.

Avoid using a masonry waterproofer on painted or coloured render. The silicone bonds to the paint rather than the masonry and adhesion is weak. Painted render needs a waterproofing additive mixed into the top coat of exterior masonry paint instead.

A well-applied cream lasts 10 to 25 years depending on the product and the exposure of the wall. Check the wall after heavy driving rain: water should bead and run off the surface. If damp patches appear within two years, the original application was on wet or contaminated masonry and needs to be repeated on a properly dry wall. Reapply on the manufacturer's specified cycle or when the water-beading effect is no longer visible.

attrs.fix: Check for a continuous damp line at the same height as the external ground level (rising damp) or for cold surfaces and ventilation problems (condensation). Both need a different remedy. The cream has not made anything worse.
attrs.cause: The source of damp is not rain penetration but rising damp, condensation, or a failed DPC. Waterproofing cream addresses rain penetration only.
attrs.symptom: Damp patches still appear on the inside of the wall after treatment

attrs.fix: Allow the wall to dry fully. Light white residue weathers off in one to two months. Heavy pooling can be carefully scrubbed back with warm water and a stiff brush before it sets.
attrs.cause: Applied too thickly or applied to brick that was not fully dry; the silicone has pooled on the surface rather than soaking in.
attrs.symptom: The cream left visible white streaks or a blotchy finish on the brickwork

attrs.fix: Locate the entry point by checking the wall during rain with a torch held at a low angle. Seal the crack or repoint the failed joint, allow to dry, then apply a second coat of cream to the repaired area.
attrs.cause: Crack or failed pointing joint that was not sealed before treatment.
attrs.symptom: Water no longer beads on the wall but interior damp has returned within a year

PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
